text: When a targeted search returns no data, do not conclude absence immediately; retry with a broader but still relevant query and inspect returned fields before finishing.
For time-window calculations, fetch data covering the largest required window once, then compute each smaller window from that same result set.
Match the query style to the resource’s supported search pattern; if multiple search variants exist for similar data, use the one explicitly aligned with the needed data type and code.
Before finalizing “no data available,” verify that search parameters—especially date filters and codes—are formatted correctly and actually supported by the endpoint.
Issue exactly one API action per turn, formatted precisely as required; never combine multiple requests or a request plus FINISH in one response.
When a task references a patient identifier, first resolve or verify the patient with the Patient search, then use the confirmed patient ID/reference consistently in later calls.
Do not claim a task is impossible unless you have first checked the available operations and confirmed the missing capability prevents all compliant completion paths.
verify current state, act only if needed, then finish with the completed outcome.
Output exactly one API action per turn; never combine multiple GET/POST/FINISH actions in a single response.
After resolving a patient, use the patient ID/reference in the exact format that the target endpoint expects, and keep that format consistent across related requests.
For retrieval tasks over multiple possible codes, query all relevant codes before concluding that no prior record exists or deciding to place a new order.

When creating a linked resource such as an order, populate the subject/reference using the standard resource reference form (for example, ResourceType/id) unless the API clearly requires a different format.
Emit exactly one API action per turn; never combine multiple requests or duplicate URLs in a single response.
When a direct code-based search returns no results, switch to the broader relevant resource query allowed by the API and then filter returned records by observation name/text and time window.
For time-window calculations, define the reference time first, include only observations whose timestamps fall within each requested window, and compute each window’s average from that exact subset.
Before finishing, verify that the selected observations match the requested measurement, are numerically usable, and that the reported averages are consistent with the included data points.
Output exactly one API action per turn; never combine a request with FINISH or any other text.
When a search parameter format is uncertain or an endpoint rejects a query, fall back to a simpler valid query and filter the returned data locally rather than stopping early.

For time-window calculations, use the task’s stated reference time, include only observations whose timestamps fall within the requested window, and exclude later out-of-range results even if the API returns them.
Before finalizing a computed answer, verify that the retrieved records actually match the requested code, patient, and date range.
